reflection_text: This week's lecture covered the causes and consequences of drug shortages in depth. I learned that shortages are driven by factors including limited manufacturing capacity, quality failures such as contamination at production facilities, over-reliance on single suppliers for active pharmaceutical ingredients, and low market profitability for generic medications — particularly sterile injectables. I also learned that pharmacists play a central role in shortage management, including identifying therapeutic alternatives and supporting ethical allocation decisions when supply is critically limited. This is important because drug shortages directly affect patient care, and understanding the root causes helps me see shortages as a systemic issue rather than random bad luck. Knowing this will be relevant to my future career, since I may regularly encounter situations where first-line medications are unavailable. In the future, I will try to stay informed about current drug shortages and work with providers to find appropriate alternatives when medications are unavailable. I hope to apply what I learned in my upcoming rotations.

I also learned that pharmacists play a central role in shortage management, including identifying therapeutic alternatives and supporting ethical allocation decisions when supply is critically limited.

Knowing this will be relevant to my future career, since I may regularly encounter situations where first-line medications are unavailable.

reflection_text: This week I learned about the causes and consequences of drug shortages in the United States. Shortages are primarily driven by market forces — particularly the low profitability of generic drug manufacturing — which discourages investment in production capacity and quality systems. When a quality problem such as a contamination event occurs, manufacturers in a low-margin industry often lack the resources to remediate quickly, prolonging the shortage. I also learned about categories of drugs most susceptible to shortage, including sterile injectables and oncology medications, and about the ASHP shortage management guidelines that pharmacists use to guide their response. Understanding the root causes of drug shortages is important for my development as a pharmacist because it helps me anticipate supply problems and understand why they happen. It is relevant to know that shortages are a systemic and recurring issue, not just rare emergencies, and this will make me a more informed and prepared professional.

When a quality problem such as a contamination event occurs, manufacturers in a low-margin industry often lack the resources to remediate quickly, prolonging the shortage. I also learned about categories of drugs most susceptible to shortage, including sterile injectables and oncology medications, and about the ASHP shortage management guidelines that pharmacists use to guide their response.

reflection_text: This week's presentation gave me a much clearer picture of why drug shortages occur and how far-reaching their consequences can be. I learned that shortages rarely have a single cause; instead, they result from overlapping vulnerabilities in the pharmaceutical supply chain — including heavy API sourcing from a limited number of overseas facilities, the economics of generic drug production that discourage manufacturers from maintaining excess capacity, and the slow recovery time following FDA quality enforcement actions. I also learned about the categories of medications most at risk, particularly sterile injectables and narrow-therapeutic-index drugs, and about the tools pharmacists use to respond, including therapeutic interchange protocols and shortage allocation frameworks. Before this lecture, I had not appreciated how structural the shortage problem is. I assumed shortages were caused primarily by external shocks like natural disasters or pandemics — and while those events do contribute, the lecture helped me see that the underlying fragility already exists, and those events simply expose it. This was a meaningful shift in perspective because it means shortages are partially predictable, and pharmacists who understand the system can act proactively rather than just reactively. This also connected to an experience I had during my community pharmacy IPPE, when my preceptor had to navigate a backorder on a patient's thyroid medication — something I did not fully understand at the time but can now place within a larger systemic picture. Going forward, I want to be more proactive about staying informed on current drug shortages. I know that databases like the ASHP Drug Shortages Resource Center exist and I plan to consult them regularly in practice.

I also learned about the categories of medications most at risk, particularly sterile injectables and narrow-therapeutic-index drugs, and about the tools pharmacists use to respond, including therapeutic interchange protocols and shortage allocation frameworks.
evidence_why: This was a meaningful shift in perspective because it means shortages are partially predictable, and pharmacists who understand the system can act proactively rather than just reactively. This also connected to an experience I had during my community pharmacy IPPE, when my preceptor had to navigate a backorder on a patient's thyroid medication — something I did not fully understand at the time but can now place within a larger systemic picture.

reflection_text:
This week we discussed how pharmacists should communicate with patients when a medication is on shortage. The main idea is that patients need to be told clearly when their medication is being substituted, why, and what the differences are. We talked about how silent substitutions damage trust and can lead patients to stop taking their medication or look for it elsewhere.

This is important because communication during a shortage is part of patient safety, not just an inconvenience. If patients don't understand the change, they may not take the new medication correctly. At my internship I will try to be clear with patients when there are substitutions and explain what they need to know.

reflection_text: In this week's session, we discussed high-alert medications and how errors can happen at different points along the way. We talked about how hospitals try to reduce these risks using different strategies. This learning matters to me because it changed my understanding of where medication safety actually lives. I used to think about safety as happening at the final step — the pharmacist checking the order or the nurse administering the drug — but this session helped me realize that errors are often introduced much earlier, at the prescribing or transcribing stage, and the later steps are just the last chance to catch them. This is important for my development because it shifts my mental model from "catch errors at the end" to "design each step to make errors less likely in the first place." It also makes me more aware that my own future decisions as a pharmacist — the order sets I use, the clarifications I ask for, how I communicate with nurses — are all part of a shared system. I want to approach HAMs with that systems mindset rather than relying on individual carefulness. In the future, I will try to be mindful of HAMs when I see them at work.
evidence_what: we discussed high-alert medications and how errors can happen at different points along the way. We talked about how hospitals try to reduce these risks using different strategies.
evidence_why: I used to think about safety as happening at the final step — the pharmacist checking the order or the nurse administering the drug — but this session helped me realize that errors are often introduced much earlier, at the prescribing or transcribing stage, and the later steps are just the last chance to catch them.
evidence_how: In the future, I will try to be mindful of HAMs when I see them at work.

reflection_text: In today's session on high-alert medications (HAMs), I learned that look-alike/sound-alike (LASA) confusion and packaging-related failures contribute to a substantial share of HAM errors. ISMP and the FDA publish lists of confusable name pairs — such as hydralazine vs. hydroxyzine and DOBUTamine vs. DOPamine — and recommend mitigations including tall-man lettering, physical separation of storage, distinct auxiliary labeling, and purchasing from multiple manufacturers so that similar-looking vials do not sit side by side on the shelf. This matters for my development because it illustrates that "being careful" is not sufficient protection against errors that are built into the physical environment. I had not previously thought about a vial label or a storage bin location as safety interventions, but the session made me see that these are actually high-leverage levers because they reduce reliance on memory and vigilance. That reframes the pharmacist's role — we are partly human-factors designers, and every choice about storage, labeling, and workflow is a safety decision. It also makes me more humble about my own susceptibility to LASA errors, because I now understand that I can be well-trained and still mix up two similar names if the system sets me up to. In my future practice, I want to be thoughtful about LASA medications and do my part to reduce these kinds of errors.

reflection_text: Today we covered Just Culture and how healthcare organizations respond to medication errors depending on the behavior involved. We went over the three categories of human error, at-risk behavior, and reckless behavior, and how each one calls for a different response (consoling, coaching, or discipline). We also discussed how blame-based cultures discourage reporting of near misses, while Just Culture encourages staff to speak up. This topic is important to me because I used to assume all medication errors deserved the same consequence. Realizing that intent and system context matter has changed how I think about accountability in the pharmacy. It also made me reflect on how many near misses probably go unreported because staff are afraid of being blamed, and how that fear ends up hurting future patients by keeping broken systems in place. In my internship, I want to be more open when I make mistakes or see them happen.

reflection_text: Today we talked about the different terms used to describe safety events in healthcare, including near misses, errors, and adverse events. We also touched on how some events are considered more serious and get reported and reviewed more formally. This learning is important because I realized I have been using the word 'error' for everything, and that imprecision can affect how a problem is investigated and ultimately fixed. It also made me reflect on the value of near misses. At the pharmacy where I work, near misses happen often and most of them get mentioned to a coworker and then forgotten. I now see that each one of those is a warning sign that we are ignoring, which is humbling. Going forward, I want to pay more attention to safety events in my workplace.

reflection_text: In today's class, we covered the standards that apply to sterile compounding in pharmacy. I learned that USP sets guidelines that pharmacies must follow when preparing sterile products. These standards cover things like cleanroom requirements and procedures for ensuring sterility. Understanding these requirements was important for me because it shifted how I think about compounding as a career path. Before today, I thought compounding was just about making personalized medications, but now I see it as a highly regulated, patient safety-critical practice. A single failure in the sterile environment can lead to contaminated products, patient infections, or even death--events that have actually occurred due to compounding pharmacy failures. This made me appreciate why sterile compounders must be extremely meticulous. I also realized that my initial assumption--that compounding was a relatively unregulated niche area of pharmacy--was completely wrong. In the future, I plan to apply these standards at my internship and beyond.

A single failure in the sterile environment can lead to contaminated products, patient infections, or even death--events that have actually occurred due to compounding pharmacy failures. This made me appreciate why sterile compounders must be extremely meticulous. I also realized that my initial assumption--that compounding was a relatively unregulated niche area of pharmacy--was completely wrong.

reflection_text: Today's lesson was about patient privacy and HIPAA. HIPAA is a law that protects patient health information in healthcare settings, including pharmacies. It applies to pharmacists and other healthcare providers who handle patient information. Patient privacy is something all pharmacists need to respect.
evidence_what: Today's lesson was about patient privacy and HIPAA. HIPAA is a law that protects patient health information in healthcare settings, including pharmacies.
evidence_why: Patient privacy is something all pharmacists need to respect.
evidence_how: It applies to pharmacists and other healthcare providers who handle patient information.

reflection_text: Today I learned that HIPAA--the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act--is composed of two key rules especially relevant to pharmacy practice: the Privacy Rule, which governs the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI), and the Security Rule, which applies specifically to electronic PHI (ePHI). PHI is defined as individually identifiable health information linked to any of 18 specific identifiers--such as name, address, dates of service, prescription numbers, or biometric data--that relates to a patient's health status, care, or payment for care. I learned that pharmacists must apply the minimum necessary standard when sharing patient information, meaning only the information required for the specific task at hand should be disclosed. HIPAA permits disclosure of PHI without patient authorization for Treatment, Payment, and Healthcare Operations (TPO), but other disclosures generally require a signed patient authorization. Violations carry tiered civil monetary penalties ranging from $100 to $50,000 per violation depending on culpability, as well as potential criminal liability for willful disclosures. This is important for my development as a pharmacist because patients must trust that their health information is handled with care. Without that trust, patients may withhold information that is critical to their safe care--putting themselves at risk. I will make sure to follow HIPAA standards when I work in pharmacy settings and be careful about how I share patient information.
evidence_what: HIPAA--the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act--is composed of two key rules especially relevant to pharmacy practice: the Privacy Rule, which governs the use and disclosure of Protected Health Information (PHI), and the Security Rule, which applies specifically to electronic PHI (ePHI). PHI is defined as individually identifiable health information linked to any of 18 specific identifiers--such as name, address, dates of service, prescription numbers, or biometric data--that relates to a patient's health status, care, or payment for care.
evidence_why: This is important for my development as a pharmacist because patients must trust that their health information is handled with care. Without that trust, patients may withhold information that is critical to their safe care--putting themselves at risk.
evidence_how: I will make sure to follow HIPAA standards when I work in pharmacy settings and be careful about how I share patient information.

reflection_text: Today we discussed regulations that govern the safety and legitimacy of drug products as they move through the supply chain from manufacturers to pharmacies to patients. These standards help ensure that medications arriving at pharmacies can be traced back to their origin and verified as legitimate. This is important because it prevents counterfeit or diverted drugs from reaching patients, which could cause serious or life-threatening harm. At my internship, I will pay attention to how drug shipments are received and verified at my site.

evidence_why: This is important because it prevents counterfeit or diverted drugs from reaching patients, which could cause serious or life-threatening harm.
evidence_how: At my internship, I will pay attention to how drug shipments are received and verified at my site.

I will keep this concept in mind during my internship.

reflection_text: Today's session covered safety huddles and structured communication tools like SBAR. These are approaches used by healthcare teams to proactively identify risks and communicate concerns in a standardized way. Safety huddles are short daily meetings where teams surface potential problems before they become errors, and SBAR is a communication format that ensures key information is conveyed clearly when handing off patients or raising concerns. This is important because poor communication is one of the leading contributors to medication errors and adverse events in healthcare. SBAR especially matters to me as a future pharmacist because I will need to escalate drug therapy concerns to physicians and nurses, and having a clear structure increases the chance that my concerns are heard and addressed. I am not yet sure how much I will be able to participate in formal huddles during my internship, but I plan to use SBAR whenever I communicate a clinical concern verbally.
